why_winner: The split-screen opening is the sharpest hook of the three — it delivers the entire thesis in two lines before a reader can scroll past. Performance data shows comparison framing outperforms story-first with this audience, and Variant C puts the comparison front and center rather than earning it through narrative. The 'neither is wrong' landing also avoids preachiness better than A or B.

runner_up_notes.B: The phrase 'you're maintaining a living system, not shipping a stable artifact' is excellent — crisper than C's version of the same idea. Worth grafting into the revision.
runner_up_notes.A: The line 'You never fully own an AI agent the way you own a function you wrote' is authentic and personal — consider borrowing it for a mid-post moment in the revision.

issues.description: The vendor dimension ('company that owns the model can change it') is introduced abruptly mid-post and exits quickly. It deserves one more line to land — it's actually the scariest part of the argument.
issues.action: After 'New ones appear.' add a single punchy sentence that makes the reader feel the loss of control — e.g. borrowing B's framing: 'You're maintaining a living system, not shipping a stable artifact.'

issues.description: The opening two-line contrast is strong but the second line could be sharpened — 'maintain it forever' reads slightly hyperbolic.
issues.action: Consider: 'AI agents: write it once, then keep tending it.' — preserves the contrast but grounds it in the real experience of ongoing attention rather than 'forever'.
